standards.title: Authenticator Assurance Level 1 (AAL1)
standards.description: AAL1 provides some assurance that the claimant controls an authenticator bound to the subscriber account. AAL1 requires single-factor authentication using a wide range of available authentication technologies. Successful authentication requires that the claimant prove possession and control of the authenticator through a secure authentication protocol. Replay resistance is not required at this level, but eavesdropper resistance is required for all authenticator types.

standards.title: Authenticator Assurance Level 2 (AAL2)
standards.description: AAL2 provides high confidence that the claimant controls authenticator(s) bound to the subscriber account. Proof of possession and control of two distinct authentication factors is required through secure authentication protocol(s). AAL2 mandates multi-factor authentication, requiring a combination of something you know, something you have, or something you are. Approved cryptographic techniques are required for all authenticator types, and replay resistance is mandatory. Session binding must use authenticated protected channels.

standards.title: Authenticator Assurance Level 3 (AAL3)
standards.description: AAL3 provides very high confidence that the claimant controls authenticator(s) bound to the subscriber account. Authentication at AAL3 is based on proof of possession of a key through a cryptographic protocol. AAL3 authentication SHALL use a hardware-based authenticator and an authenticator that provides verifier impersonation resistance — this means phishing-resistant authentication is mandatory. The authenticator SHALL be a multi-factor cryptographic device or used in combination with another factor. All communication must occur over authenticated protected channels with mutual authentication.

standards.title: Identity Assurance Level 1 (IAL1)
standards.description: IAL1 requires that the applicant provide a self-asserted identity claim to the CSP. There is no requirement for identity evidence collection, validation, or verification at this level. The CSP is not required to verify the real-world existence of the claimed identity. IAL1 is suitable when the identity of the individual is not critical to the service being provided, but a unique handle or pseudonym is needed for accountability purposes.

standards.title: Identity Assurance Level 2 (IAL2)
standards.description: IAL2 introduces the need for either remote or in-person identity proofing. The CSP must collect identity evidence (e.g., government-issued ID) and validate its authenticity and accuracy. Verification of the identity is required, binding the applicant to the claimed real-world identity. Evidence must be verified through authoritative or credible sources, and the CSP shall verify the binding of the applicant to the claimed identity through comparison of physical characteristics or biometric comparison. Address confirmation is required either through an enrollment code sent to a confirmed address or through two pieces of SUPERIOR or STRONG evidence.

standards.title: Identity Assurance Level 3 (IAL3)
standards.description: IAL3 requires in-person or supervised remote identity proofing. The CSP must verify the identity of the applicant by physically comparing them to their identity evidence or performing a biometric comparison. A trained operator must be involved in the identity proofing process. IAL3 requires SUPERIOR evidence to be verified against the issuing source. All steps of the proofing process must be witnessed by the CSP or trusted representative. Address confirmation is mandatory and fraud detection measures must be in place.

standards.title: Federation Assurance Level 1 (FAL1)
standards.description: FAL1 allows the RP to receive a bearer assertion from the IdP. The assertion must be signed by the IdP and contain, at minimum, a reference to the subscriber and an expiration time. The assertion may be transmitted through the front channel (browser redirect) or back channel (server-to-server). At FAL1, the bearer nature of the assertion means anyone in possession of it can use it, making eavesdropping protection critical. TLS is required for all assertion transmissions.

standards.title: Federation Assurance Level 2 (FAL2)
standards.description: FAL2 adds the requirement that the assertion be encrypted to a key held by the RP, in addition to being signed by the IdP. This means the assertion is a holder-of-key assertion — the RP must demonstrate possession of a cryptographic key referenced in the assertion. Even if the assertion is intercepted, it cannot be used by an unauthorized party because the RP must prove key possession. Back-channel presentation is preferred at this level to limit exposure of the assertion to the subscriber's browser.

standards.title: Federation Assurance Level 3 (FAL3)
standards.description: FAL3 requires a bound authenticator in addition to all FAL2 protections. The subscriber must prove possession of a cryptographic key bound to the assertion at the RP, creating a direct trust chain from the subscriber through the IdP to the RP. This prevents assertion replay and injection attacks even if both the assertion and RP key are compromised, because the attacker would also need the subscriber's bound authenticator. FAL3 effectively creates a three-party proof that the subscriber, IdP, and RP are all mutually authenticated.

standards.title: Memorized Secret Authenticators (Passwords)
standards.description: Memorized secrets (passwords, PINs) SHALL be at least 8 characters when chosen by the subscriber, and at least 6 characters when randomly assigned by the CSP. The CSP SHALL require a maximum length of no fewer than 64 characters. All printing ASCII characters and Unicode SHALL be accepted. The verifier SHALL compare the prospective secret against a blocklist of compromised/common values. No composition rules (e.g., requiring special characters) SHALL be imposed. No periodic password changes shall be mandated unless there is evidence of compromise. Rate limiting or increasing delays SHALL be implemented against brute force attacks.

standards.title: Look-Up Secret Authenticators
standards.description: Look-up secrets are pre-generated lists of secrets (e.g., recovery codes) shared between the CSP and subscriber. Each secret SHALL be used only once. The verifier SHALL store look-up secrets using an approved one-way function (salted hash). The authenticator SHALL have at least 20 bits of entropy per secret if the verifier uses a look-up table, or 112 bits if secrets are individually hashed. Look-up secrets are typically used as backup authenticators for account recovery scenarios.

standards.title: Out-of-Band Authenticators
standards.description: Out-of-band authenticators operate through a separate communication channel from the primary authentication channel. The out-of-band device SHALL be uniquely addressable and communication over the secondary channel SHALL be encrypted. The verifier SHALL generate a random secret with at least 20 bits of entropy and transmit it via the secondary channel. PSTN (SMS/voice) is restricted — organizations SHALL use alternative methods where possible due to demonstrated vulnerabilities in SMS delivery. Push notifications with a numbered code comparison are the preferred method for out-of-band authentication.

standards.title: Single-Factor OTP Authenticators
standards.description: Single-factor OTP devices generate one-time passwords using a symmetric key stored on the device combined with a nonce (typically time-based or event-based). The OTP SHALL have at least 6 digits (approximately 20 bits of entropy). Time-based OTPs (TOTP) SHALL use a time step of 30 seconds, and the verifier SHALL accept OTPs within a reasonable clock drift window. The symmetric key SHALL be generated using an approved random bit generator and stored securely on the device. These authenticators provide a single authentication factor (something you have).

standards.title: Multi-Factor OTP Authenticators
standards.description: Multi-factor OTP authenticators require activation through a second factor (e.g., PIN or biometric) before generating the OTP. The device combines an embedded symmetric key with the activation factor and a nonce to produce the OTP. The OTP SHALL have at least 6 digits. The activation secret or biometric sample SHALL be collected and processed within a secure boundary. These devices provide two authentication factors in a single interaction: something you have (the device) and something you know (PIN) or something you are (biometric). Suitable for AAL2 as a single authenticator.

standards.title: Single-Factor Cryptographic Device Authenticators
standards.description: Single-factor cryptographic devices use a private key stored on the device to authenticate by signing a challenge from the verifier. The private key SHALL be stored in suitably secure hardware (e.g., TPM, secure element) that prevents export. The device operates without requiring a second activation factor. The cryptographic operations SHALL use approved algorithms with a minimum security strength of 112 bits. These authenticators provide strong proof of possession but only a single factor, making them suitable for AAL1 or AAL2 when combined with another factor.

standards.title: Multi-Factor Cryptographic Device Authenticators
standards.description: Multi-factor cryptographic device authenticators require activation through a second factor (PIN, biometric) before performing cryptographic operations. The private key is stored in a hardware cryptographic module that prevents key export. The device signs a challenge from the verifier only after successful activation. FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware security keys are the canonical example of this authenticator type. These authenticators provide verifier impersonation resistance and are the only single-authenticator option for AAL3 because they combine something you have (hardware key), something you know/are (PIN/biometric), and cryptographic proof of possession.

standards.title: Multi-Factor Cryptographic Software Authenticators
standards.description: Multi-factor cryptographic software authenticators use a private key stored in software (e.g., disk, software keystore) that requires activation through a second factor before use. Unlike hardware-backed authenticators, the key may be more vulnerable to extraction through malware or forensic analysis. The software SHALL use approved cryptographic algorithms and the activation factor SHALL be collected within a trusted execution environment where possible. These authenticators satisfy AAL2 when used alone but cannot satisfy AAL3 because the key is not in tamper-resistant hardware.

standards.title: Verifier Requirements for Memorized Secrets
standards.description: Verifiers SHALL store memorized secrets using a suitable one-way key derivation function that is salted and computationally expensive (e.g., PBKDF2, bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2). The salt SHALL be at least 32 bits in length and chosen randomly. The key derivation function SHALL use an approved one-way function with an iteration count or memory cost calibrated to introduce a minimum of 100ms delay. The verifier SHALL NOT truncate the secret before hashing. Verifiers SHALL implement rate limiting to protect against online guessing attacks, locking the account after a maximum of 100 consecutive failed attempts.

standards.title: Verifier Requirements for Biometric Authentication
standards.description: Biometrics SHALL be used only as an activation factor for a multi-factor authenticator, not as a standalone authentication method. The biometric system SHALL have a false match rate (FMR) of 1 in 1000 or better and SHALL implement presentation attack detection. Biometric comparison SHALL be performed locally on the claimant's device or within a secure boundary — biometric templates should not be transmitted to the verifier. The system SHALL allow no more than 5 consecutive failed biometric attempts before requiring an alternative authentication method. Biometric data must be protected with the same rigor as other authentication secrets.

standards.title: Session Binding Requirements
standards.description: Session secrets SHALL be generated using an approved random bit generator with at least 64 bits of entropy. The session secret SHALL be transmitted over an authenticated protected channel (TLS). Session secrets SHALL be bound to the subscriber's session and SHALL NOT be reusable across sessions. The verifier SHALL associate the session with the subscriber's account and authentication event. For AAL2 and above, session secrets SHALL be wrapped in a TLS session and bound to the TLS master secret to prevent session hijacking through cookie theft.

standards.title: Session Timeout and Reauthentication
standards.description: Sessions SHALL time out after a period of inactivity defined by the CSP based on a risk assessment. At AAL1, reauthentication SHALL occur at least every 30 days. At AAL2, reauthentication is required every 12 hours or 30 minutes of inactivity, whichever comes first. At AAL3, reauthentication is required every 12 hours or 15 minutes of inactivity. When a session times out, the subscriber SHALL be required to reauthenticate at the originally authenticated AAL. The CSP MAY prompt the subscriber for reauthentication before the session expires to improve usability.

standards.title: Reauthentication from Federation or Assertion
standards.description: When reauthentication is triggered for a session originally established through federation, the RP SHALL communicate the reauthentication requirement to the IdP. The IdP SHALL reauthenticate the subscriber at the originally asserted AAL. If the IdP is unable to reauthenticate the subscriber at the required level, the session SHALL be terminated. The RP SHALL NOT accept a new assertion that does not reflect a current, active authentication event. This ensures that federated sessions maintain the same assurance level throughout their lifetime.

standards.title: Verifier Impersonation Resistance (Phishing Resistance)
standards.description: Authenticators that provide verifier impersonation resistance use a cryptographic protocol that binds the authenticator output to the specific verifier being authenticated to. This means the output of the authentication protocol is useless if replayed to a different verifier. Cryptographic device authenticators that use public-key cryptography with channel binding (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn with TLS channel binding) inherently provide this property. Verifier impersonation resistance is REQUIRED at AAL3 and is the strongest defense against phishing attacks because even a perfectly crafted phishing site cannot capture a reusable credential.

standards.title: Authentication Replay Resistance
standards.description: Replay resistance ensures that a captured authentication message cannot be successfully replayed at a later time. At AAL2 and above, the authentication protocol SHALL provide replay resistance. This is typically achieved through the use of nonces, timestamps, or challenge-response protocols. OTP authenticators provide inherent replay resistance because each OTP is valid only once or for a very short time window. Cryptographic authenticators provide replay resistance through challenge-response protocols where the challenge includes a verifier-generated nonce.

standards.title: Rate Limiting and Throttling Requirements
standards.description: The verifier SHALL implement controls to protect against online guessing attacks on memorized secrets. Effective rate limiting SHALL limit the rate of authentication attempts to no more than 100 failed consecutive attempts on a single account. The verifier SHALL employ increasingly progressive delays (e.g., exponential backoff), CAPTCHA challenges, IP-based throttling, or temporary account lockout after successive failed attempts. When an account is locked, the subscriber SHALL be able to unlock it through an out-of-band mechanism. These measures collectively protect against brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.

standards.title: Authenticator Lifecycle Management
standards.description: The CSP SHALL maintain a record of all authenticators associated with each subscriber account. Authenticator binding SHALL be performed at the same or higher AAL as the credential being bound. When an authenticator is lost, stolen, or compromised, the CSP SHALL provide mechanisms for timely revocation. The CSP SHALL support the binding of additional authenticators after initial enrollment to support account recovery and multi-device usage. Authenticator renewal or re-binding SHALL require authentication at the AAL of the credential being renewed. All authenticator lifecycle events SHALL be logged for audit purposes.

standards.title: Authenticator Revocation
standards.description: The CSP SHALL provide a mechanism for revoking authenticators when they are reported lost, stolen, or compromised. Revocation SHALL take effect as soon as practical but no later than the next authentication attempt. The subscriber SHALL be notified of the revocation through an independent communication channel. After revocation, the CSP SHALL provide a secure mechanism for the subscriber to bind a new authenticator. The revocation of one authenticator SHALL NOT affect other authenticators bound to the same account unless there is evidence of broader account compromise.

standards.title: Remote Identity Proofing Requirements
standards.description: Remote identity proofing allows the CSP to verify an applicant's identity without requiring physical presence. At IAL2, the CSP SHALL collect at least one piece of SUPERIOR evidence or two pieces of STRONG evidence, and validate them against authoritative sources. The CSP SHALL verify the binding between the applicant and the identity evidence through comparison of a photograph on the evidence with the applicant's live photo or video. Liveness detection SHALL be employed to mitigate presentation attacks. The entire remote proofing session SHALL be encrypted and SHALL include fraud detection analytics to identify synthetic or manipulated evidence.

standards.title: In-Person Identity Proofing Requirements
standards.description: In-person identity proofing at IAL3 requires the applicant to appear before a trusted agent of the CSP. The trusted agent SHALL physically inspect identity evidence for signs of tampering or forgery. The agent SHALL compare the applicant's physical appearance to the photograph on the identity evidence. At least one piece of SUPERIOR evidence must be verified against the issuing source. The CSP SHALL maintain a chain of custody for all evidence examined. All interactions SHALL be logged, including the identity of the trusted agent, the evidence presented, and the verification results. Supervised remote proofing using real-time audio-video communication may be used as an alternative when it provides comparable security.

standards.title: Assertion Protection Requirements
standards.description: Assertions SHALL be digitally signed by the IdP using an approved algorithm. At FAL2 and above, assertions SHALL additionally be encrypted to a key controlled by the RP. Assertions SHALL contain an audience restriction limiting their use to the intended RP. Assertions SHALL include an issuance timestamp and expiration time, with a validity window no longer than necessary (typically 5 minutes for bearer assertions). The assertion SHALL contain a unique identifier to enable the RP to detect replay. Assertions SHALL identify the IdP, the subscriber, the RP, and the authentication event, including the AAL at which the subscriber was authenticated.

standards.title: Attribute Disclosure and Consent in Federation
standards.description: The IdP SHALL provide the subscriber with notice of the attributes being transmitted to the RP and SHALL obtain the subscriber's consent before releasing them. The IdP SHALL support selective disclosure, allowing the subscriber to choose which attributes to release. The IdP SHALL maintain a record of attribute disclosures for audit purposes. Pairwise pseudonymous identifiers SHALL be used when the RP does not require knowledge of the subscriber's real identity. These privacy-preserving measures ensure federation does not become a vehicle for unauthorized data collection while maintaining accountability.

standards.title: Back-Channel Assertion Presentation
standards.description: In back-channel presentation, the IdP delivers an assertion reference (not the full assertion) to the subscriber's browser, which forwards it to the RP. The RP then contacts the IdP directly through a server-to-server call to retrieve the full assertion. This approach limits the exposure of the assertion to the subscriber's browser and provides stronger protection against assertion theft. The assertion reference SHALL be single-use, time-limited, and SHALL be bound to the specific RP that will present it. Back-channel presentation is preferred at FAL2 and above because it significantly reduces the attack surface for assertion interception.

standards.title: Identity Evidence Strength Classification
standards.description: Identity evidence is classified into strength categories: UNACCEPTABLE, WEAK, FAIR, STRONG, and SUPERIOR. STRONG evidence (e.g., government-issued photo ID) must contain the applicant's name and photo, be unexpired, and contain security features that make forgery difficult. SUPERIOR evidence (e.g., a passport with an embedded RFID chip) must additionally be issued through processes that confirm the real-world existence of the applicant and contain cryptographic features that enable machine-readable validation of authenticity. The CSP SHALL assess the strength of evidence presented and ensure it meets the minimum requirements for the target IAL.

standards.title: Cryptographic Authenticator Key Management
standards.description: Cryptographic authenticator keys SHALL be generated using an approved random bit generator with a minimum security strength of 112 bits. Private keys SHALL be stored in a manner that limits their exposure — hardware-backed storage (TPM, secure element, HSM) is preferred. Keys SHALL have a defined lifecycle including generation, distribution, storage, use, and destruction phases. Key compromise SHALL trigger immediate revocation of the associated authenticator. The verifier SHALL verify that the public key presented during authentication matches the key originally registered during authenticator binding. Key pair generation SHOULD occur on the authenticator itself rather than externally to prevent key exposure during distribution.

standards.title: Selecting Assurance Levels (xAL Selection)
standards.description: Organizations SHALL perform a risk assessment to determine the appropriate combination of IAL, AAL, and FAL for each digital service. The selection SHALL consider the potential impact of authentication errors (false accept/false reject) and identity proofing errors on the organization, individuals, and other parties. Higher assurance levels are warranted when unauthorized access could result in significant harm, financial loss, or privacy breach. The selected levels SHALL be documented in the system security plan and reviewed periodically. Organizations SHOULD select the lowest assurance level that adequately mitigates the assessed risk to avoid unnecessary user friction and implementation cost.

standards.title: Authentication Intent Requirement
standards.description: At AAL2 and above, the verifier SHALL establish authentication intent — that is, the subscriber SHALL take an explicit action to authenticate (e.g., pressing a button, entering a PIN, performing a biometric gesture). Automatic authentication without subscriber interaction (e.g., proximity-based authentication that triggers without user action) does not satisfy the intent requirement. This prevents unauthorized authentication through stolen devices or tokens that might be in proximity to a verifier. The intent mechanism SHALL be clearly distinguishable from normal device usage to prevent the subscriber from unknowingly authenticating to a malicious verifier.

standards.title: Restricted Authenticator Usage
standards.description: NIST designates certain authenticator types as 'restricted' when they have known vulnerabilities or limitations. Currently, PSTN-based out-of-band authenticators (SMS OTP, voice OTP) are classified as restricted due to demonstrated weaknesses in the PSTN network (SIM swapping, SS7 attacks, carrier interception). When a restricted authenticator is used at AAL2, the CSP SHALL offer at least one non-restricted alternative, SHALL implement additional security controls (e.g., binding to a specific phone number verified through carrier records), and SHALL provide clear notice to the subscriber of the risks. If use of restricted authenticators is discontinued, the transition plan SHALL be communicated well in advance.

standards.title: Account Recovery and Authenticator Replacement
standards.description: Account recovery SHALL be performed at the same or higher AAL as the original credential. The CSP SHALL NOT use knowledge-based authentication (security questions) as the sole recovery mechanism. At AAL2 and above, recovery SHALL require demonstration of possession of an enrolled authenticator or completion of identity proofing at the same or higher IAL. If all authenticators are lost, the subscriber SHALL be required to repeat the identity proofing process. Recovery codes, if issued, SHALL be generated with at least 112 bits of entropy and SHALL be stored as salted hashes by the CSP. The CSP SHALL rate-limit recovery attempts to prevent abuse.

standards.title: IdP and RP Registration in Federation
standards.description: Before participating in a federation, the IdP and RP SHALL mutually register with each other or with a common federation authority. Registration SHALL establish the cryptographic keys or certificates used for assertion signing and encryption. The IdP SHALL maintain a registry of approved RPs including their assertion endpoint URLs, public keys, and authorized attribute sets. RPs SHALL validate that assertions originate from the expected IdP by verifying the digital signature against the registered IdP public key. Dynamic registration SHOULD include mechanisms to verify the legitimacy of the registering party to prevent rogue IdP or RP injection attacks.

standards.title: Token Binding for Session Security
standards.description: At AAL2 and above, session tokens SHOULD be bound to the TLS connection through token binding or equivalent mechanisms. Token binding creates a cryptographic link between the session token and the TLS connection, making stolen tokens unusable on a different TLS connection. This mitigates token theft attacks including cookie theft via XSS, network interception, and session sidejacking. When token binding is not available, alternative protections such as short-lived tokens with frequent rotation, client certificate binding, or device fingerprint binding SHALL be employed. The session management system SHALL detect and reject tokens presented on a different connection than the one they were issued for.
